domains.label: Agentic Coding
domains.description: Vibe coding, CLI agents, AI IDEs

skills.name: Web browsing & search fundamentals
rubric_by_level: Has not used a web browser or search engine
Can open a browser, type a URL, and run a basic search
Uses search operators, evaluates results, and bookmarks useful pages
Teaches others effective search techniques; uses advanced search features
Builds curated research workflows combining multiple search tools
Designs information retrieval strategies and search training programs

skills.name: File management & cloud storage
rubric_by_level: Unaware of file systems or cloud storage
Can save, rename, and organize files in folders
Uses cloud storage (Google Drive, OneDrive), shares files, manages permissions
Sets up folder structures for teams; handles version conflicts
Builds automated file workflows (backup scripts, sync pipelines)
Designs org-wide file management and governance policies

skills.name: Basic spreadsheet operations
rubric_by_level: Has never used a spreadsheet
Can enter data, format cells, and do basic sums
Uses formulas (VLOOKUP, IF), sorts, filters, and creates charts
Builds complex models with pivot tables and conditional formatting
Creates automated reporting dashboards with macros or scripts
Designs spreadsheet-based systems and trains teams on data modeling

skills.name: Email & digital communication
rubric_by_level: Has not used email or messaging tools
Can send, reply, and forward emails; uses basic chat apps
Manages folders, filters, and calendar invites; uses threads effectively
Coordinates team communication; sets up shared channels and norms
Integrates email/chat with automation tools (Zapier, Power Automate)
Designs communication architectures and policies for organizations

skills.name: Password & account security basics
rubric_by_level: Unaware of password security concepts
Knows to use unique passwords and recognizes phishing basics
Uses a password manager; enables 2FA on key accounts
Advises others on account security; reviews team security practices
Implements SSO and enterprise authentication solutions
Designs security-first identity and access management systems

skills.name: Installing & using applications
rubric_by_level: Cannot install or update applications
Can download and install apps from official stores
Manages app settings, permissions, and updates across devices
Troubleshoots installation issues; manages dependencies
Packages and deploys applications for teams or clients
Designs application distribution and lifecycle management systems

skills.name: Copy/paste & keyboard shortcuts
rubric_by_level: Unaware of keyboard shortcuts
Knows copy, paste, undo, and a few OS shortcuts
Uses shortcuts fluently across multiple apps
Customizes shortcuts; uses clipboard managers and text expansion
Builds keyboard-driven workflows and automates repetitive tasks
Designs accessibility-first interaction patterns and shortcut systems

skills.name: Screen sharing & video calls
rubric_by_level: Has not used video call or screen sharing tools
Can join a video call and share their screen when guided
Manages meeting settings, recordings, breakout rooms, and annotations
Facilitates remote workshops; troubleshoots A/V issues for others
Integrates conferencing tools with other workflows (recording to summary)
Designs hybrid collaboration architectures for organizations

skills.name: Evaluating source credibility
rubric_by_level: Does not consider whether a source is trustworthy
Knows some sources are more reliable than others
Checks author credentials, publication date, and cross-references claims
Teaches source evaluation frameworks (CRAAP test, lateral reading)
Builds source credibility scoring into information workflows
Designs institutional media literacy and source evaluation programs

skills.name: Identifying misinformation patterns
rubric_by_level: Unaware of misinformation as a concept
Recognizes that misinformation exists online
Identifies common patterns: clickbait, emotional manipulation, cherry-picked data
Systematically debunks misinformation; uses fact-checking tools
Builds misinformation detection tools or automated flagging systems
Designs counter-misinformation strategies and platform-level safeguards

skills.name: Cross-referencing claims
rubric_by_level: Does not cross-reference information
Knows claims should be verified with multiple sources
Routinely checks claims against 2-3 independent sources before accepting
Uses structured verification methods; triangulates across source types
Builds automated cross-referencing pipelines for content validation
Designs verification frameworks for newsrooms or research teams

skills.name: Understanding bias in content
rubric_by_level: Unaware that content can carry bias
Recognizes that news and media can be biased
Identifies bias indicators: framing, omission, language choices
Analyzes bias across multiple viewpoints; adjusts own consumption habits
Builds bias detection tools or scoring rubrics for content review
Designs bias-aware editorial and content governance systems

skills.name: Distinguishing fact from opinion
rubric_by_level: Cannot distinguish facts from opinions
Knows the difference between a fact and an opinion
Consistently separates factual claims from editorial commentary
Teaches the distinction; applies it in professional writing and reviews
Builds content classification systems that separate fact from opinion
Designs fact/opinion labeling standards for publishing platforms

skills.name: Recognizing AI-generated content
rubric_by_level: Unaware that AI can generate text, images, or video
Knows AI-generated content exists (deepfakes, ChatGPT text)
Uses detection cues (artifacts, phrasing patterns) to identify AI content
Applies detection tools (GPTZero, metadata analysis) and teaches others
Builds AI content detection pipelines for content moderation
Designs provenance and watermarking systems for AI content authenticity

skills.name: What is AI vs traditional software
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of AI or how it differs from regular software
Can explain that AI learns from data rather than following hardcoded rules
Describes key differences: training vs programming, probabilistic vs deterministic
Explains AI vs traditional software to non-technical stakeholders with examples
Builds demonstrations or training materials comparing AI and traditional approaches
Designs curricula or frameworks for teaching AI literacy at scale

skills.name: LLM fundamentals (tokens, context, prediction)
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of LLMs, tokens, or context windows
Can explain what tokens are and that LLMs predict next tokens
Understands context window limits, token counting, and how prediction works
Optimizes prompts based on tokenization; explains context window trade-offs
Builds token-aware applications with context window management
Designs architectures that account for tokenization and context constraints

skills.name: Capabilities vs limitations (hallucinations)
rubric_by_level: Unaware of AI limitations or hallucination risk
Knows AI can make mistakes and hallucinate incorrect information
Identifies common failure modes: hallucination, outdated info, reasoning errors
Designs workflows with verification steps to catch hallucinations
Builds guardrails and automated checks for hallucination detection
Designs system-level reliability architectures accounting for all failure modes

skills.name: Model families (open vs closed)
rubric_by_level: Unaware that different AI models exist
Knows there are multiple model families (GPT, Claude, Llama, Gemini)
Compares open vs closed models on cost, privacy, and capability trade-offs
Selects models for specific tasks based on benchmarks and constraints
Builds model evaluation frameworks and switching infrastructure
Designs multi-model architectures with routing, fallback, and governance

skills.name: Reasoning vs retrieval vs tools
rubric_by_level: Unaware of how AI generates answers vs retrieves information
Knows AI can reason, retrieve from context, and use tools
Explains when to use pure reasoning vs RAG vs tool-calling approaches
Designs workflows that combine reasoning, retrieval, and tools effectively
Builds systems that dynamically choose between reasoning, retrieval, and tools
Designs decision frameworks for hybrid AI architectures at scale

skills.name: Training data & knowledge cutoffs
rubric_by_level: Unaware that models have training data or knowledge cutoffs
Knows models have a knowledge cutoff date and may lack recent info
Checks cutoff dates when prompting; provides context for recent events
Designs prompts and workflows that account for knowledge staleness
Builds systems with automatic staleness detection and live data augmentation
Designs data freshness architectures combining models with live retrieval

skills.name: Temperature & sampling basics
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of temperature or sampling in AI
Knows temperature controls randomness in model output
Adjusts temperature, top-p, and other sampling parameters for different tasks
Optimizes sampling settings per use case (creative vs factual)
Builds adaptive sampling systems that tune parameters based on context
Designs sampling strategies for production systems balancing quality and diversity

skills.name: Transformer architecture overview
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of transformers or attention
Knows transformers are the architecture behind modern LLMs
Explains self-attention, positional encoding, and why transformers scale well
Discusses architectural trade-offs (encoder-only, decoder-only, encoder-decoder)
Implements or fine-tunes transformer-based models for specific tasks
Designs novel transformer architectures or significant architectural modifications

skills.name: Diffusion models overview
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of diffusion models
Knows diffusion models power image generation (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E)
Explains the noise-to-image denoising process at a conceptual level
Compares diffusion model variants and their strengths/weaknesses
Fine-tunes or trains diffusion models for specific visual domains
Designs diffusion-based generation systems with custom pipelines

skills.name: Embeddings & semantic similarity
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of embeddings or vectors
Knows embeddings convert text/images into numerical vectors for comparison
Explains cosine similarity, embedding dimensions, and semantic search basics
Selects embedding models for specific tasks; evaluates embedding quality
Builds embedding pipelines for production (chunking, indexing, querying)
Designs embedding architectures with custom models and multi-modal indexing

skills.name: Multimodal model basics
rubric_by_level: Unaware that AI can process multiple media types
Knows some models can handle text, images, and audio together
Explains how multimodal models combine inputs (vision transformers, CLIP)
Evaluates multimodal model capabilities for specific use cases
Builds applications that leverage multimodal inputs and outputs
Designs multimodal AI architectures combining multiple models and modalities

skills.name: Privacy basics for GenAI
rubric_by_level: Unaware of privacy concerns when using AI
Knows not to paste sensitive data into public AI tools
Follows organizational data classification policies when using AI
Designs privacy guidelines for AI usage within teams or departments
Builds privacy-preserving AI workflows (data masking, on-prem models)
Designs enterprise-wide AI privacy architectures and compliance frameworks

skills.name: IP/copyright awareness
rubric_by_level: Unaware of copyright or IP issues related to AI
Knows AI-generated content may have copyright implications
Understands training data IP issues, fair use, and output ownership questions
Advises teams on IP policies for AI-generated content
Implements IP compliance checks in AI content generation workflows
Designs organizational IP and copyright policy frameworks for AI

skills.name: Bias & fairness basics
rubric_by_level: Unaware that AI systems can produce biased outputs
Knows AI can reflect biases from training data
Identifies examples of AI bias (hiring, lending, content generation)
Evaluates model outputs for bias and recommends mitigations
Builds bias detection and mitigation pipelines into AI workflows
Designs fairness frameworks and bias audit programs for AI systems

skills.name: Environmental impact awareness
rubric_by_level: Unaware that AI has environmental costs
Knows training and running AI models consumes significant energy
Can discuss carbon footprint of model training vs inference trade-offs
Recommends smaller models or efficient approaches to reduce environmental impact
Builds carbon-aware model selection and deployment pipelines
Designs sustainability strategies for large-scale AI infrastructure

skills.name: Writing clear requests to AI
rubric_by_level: Has not interacted with an AI assistant
Can type a basic question into ChatGPT/Claude and get a response
Writes clear, specific requests with context and desired output format
Coaches others on writing effective prompts; identifies common pitfalls
Builds prompt templates and libraries for team or product use
Designs prompt engineering curricula and organizational prompt standards

skills.name: Instructions + constraints + format
rubric_by_level: Unaware that prompt structure affects output quality
Knows that adding instructions and constraints improves AI responses
Consistently uses instruction + constraint + format structure in prompts
Designs complex multi-section prompts with clear formatting directives
Builds prompt systems with composable instruction/constraint/format blocks
Designs prompt architectures that separate concerns and enable reuse at scale

skills.name: Few-shot examples
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of few-shot prompting
Knows that providing examples helps the AI understand the desired output
Provides 2-5 well-chosen examples that cover edge cases
Curates example sets strategically; tests which examples improve quality most
Builds automated few-shot selection systems from example databases

skills.name: Prompt debugging & iteration
rubric_by_level: Does not iterate on prompts
Knows that prompts can be revised when output is unsatisfactory

Uses A/B prompt testing; tracks prompt versions with change notes
Builds prompt debugging and regression testing tools
Designs prompt lifecycle management systems with automated quality gates

skills.name: Role & persona prompting
rubric_by_level: Has not used role or persona prompting

Crafts effective system prompts defining persona, tone, and constraints
Designs multi-persona systems; benchmarks persona effectiveness
Builds persona management systems with version control and A/B testing

skills.name: Chain-of-thought prompting
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of chain-of-thought prompting
Knows asking AI to think step by step can improve reasoning
Uses CoT effectively for math, logic, and multi-step tasks
Designs CoT strategies for domain-specific reasoning problems
Builds systems that automatically inject CoT for complex queries

skills.name: Breaking complex tasks into steps
rubric_by_level: Does not break tasks into steps for AI
Knows complex tasks work better when broken into smaller pieces
Decomposes tasks into sequential prompts with clear handoffs
Designs multi-step prompt chains with error handling between steps
Builds automated task decomposition and chaining pipelines
Designs task orchestration architectures with dynamic step planning

skills.name: JSON/schema outputs
rubric_by_level: Has not requested structured output from AI
Knows AI can output JSON or structured formats when asked
Defines JSON schemas in prompts and validates output structure
Designs robust schema prompts with error recovery for malformed output
Builds structured output pipelines with schema validation and retry logic
Designs schema evolution strategies and structured output standards for platforms

skills.name: Rubrics as prompts
rubric_by_level: Has not used rubrics in prompts
Knows rubrics can guide AI to evaluate or score content
Creates detailed rubrics with criteria and scoring scales for AI evaluation
Designs calibrated rubrics validated against human expert ratings
Builds rubric-based evaluation systems with automated scoring pipelines

skills.name: Draft -> critique -> revise
rubric_by_level: Unaware of iterative refinement workflows with AI
Knows you can ask AI to draft, then critique, then revise
Runs structured draft to critique to revise cycles with clear criteria
Designs multi-pass refinement workflows for specific content types
Builds automated refinement pipelines with quality convergence checks

skills.name: Grounding & citations
rubric_by_level: Unaware that AI should cite sources or be grounded

skills.name: ReAct-style patterns
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of ReAct or reasoning-action patterns

skills.name: What is RAG and why use it
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of RAG or retrieval-augmented generation

Builds end-to-end RAG systems with chunking, retrieval, and generation
Designs RAG architectures for enterprise scale with quality guarantees

skills.name: Query rewriting
rubric_by_level: Unaware that user queries may need rewriting for retrieval

Designs multi-query strategies (HyDE, step-back, sub-question decomposition)

skills.name: Chunking strategies
rubric_by_level: Unaware that documents need to be split for retrieval

Builds adaptive chunking pipelines that tune size based on content structure

skills.name: Hybrid retrieval (vector + keyword)
rubric_by_level: Has not heard of hybrid retrieval

skills.name: Reranking & scoring
rubric_by_level: Unaware of reranking in search

Builds production reranking systems with latency and quality trade-offs

skills.name: Context budgeting
rubric_by_level: Unaware of context window budget constraints

skills.name: Lost in the middle mitigation

skills.name: Golden sets & regression testing

skills.name: What are AI agents
